coredata.dc:title: An amplified comparative fluorescence resonance energy transfer immunosensing of CA125 tumor marker and ovarian cancer cells using green and economic carbon dots for bio-applications in labeling, imaging and sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Hamd-Ghadareh, Somayeh
Salimi, Abdollah
Fathi, Fardin
Bahrami, Saman
coredata.dc:description: CA125, is a marker in the clinical diagnosis of several cancers and currently is the best serum-based tumor marker for ovarian cancer. Here, we developed an ultrasensitive antibody-ssDNA aptamer sandwich-type fluorescence immunosensor for CA125 detection. Based on a novel signal amplification strategy the carbon dots (CDs) functionalized with aptamer (CD-aptamer) used as detection probe and PAMAM-Dendrimers/AuNPs was used for covalent attachment of CA125-antibody and completing the sandwich assay method. By measuring of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) signals between CDs and AuNPs as nanoquenchers, the fluorescence signal quenched during sandwich complex formed between anti-CA125, CA125 and CDs-Aptamer and decreasing of fluorescence response signal is related to CA125 concentrations. Under optimal conditions, the immunosensor exhibited an extremely low calculated detection limit of 0.5fg/mL with wide linear range 1.0fg/mL to 1.0ng/mL of CA 125. The application of the immunosensor for CA125 detection in serum samples and measuring of ovarian-cancer cells was also investigated. The immunosensor revealed good sensitivity and specificity with ovarian cell concentrations from 2.5×103 to 2×104 cells/mL with correlation coefficient of 0.9937 and detection limit of 400cells/mL (4 cell in 10μL), indicating potential application of immunosensor in clinical monitoring of tumor biomarkers. Furthermore, the cell viability was not changed upon treatment with CDs probe during 24h, showing the low cytotoxicity of the probe. More importantly, CDs-antibody hybrid was achieved in selective imaging of the cancer cells over the OVCAR-3 line cells, implying its potential applications in biosensing, as well as in cancer diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Aptamer-Antibody
CA125 Immunosensor
Dendrimer-Au nanoparticles
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Ovarian-cancer cells
Cellular imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of AuNPs, and co-immobilization of PAMAM and anti-CA125 antibody onto AuNPs
Characterization and Binding studies of the engineered NPs
The FRET mechanism of CA 125 Ag detection
Optimized conditions for CA125 detection based on FRET assay
Analytical performance and specificity of the immunosensor
Determination of CA125 Ag in patient's serum samples
Detection of OVCAR-3 cancer cells with FRET assay
Cytotoxicity evaluation of the CDs nanoprobe
Imaging of CA125 Ag in OVCAR-3 cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Representative HRTEM image of CDs and the inset is TEM histogram and Gauss fitting of particle size distribution of CDs. The inserted photographs of CDs aqueous solutions under visible (left) and UV (right) light. (B) FTIR spectrum of Citric Acid and the prepared CDs. (C) Representative HRTEM image of AuNPs-PAMAM (both right side) and prepared AuNPs (both left side) and their related size distribution were inserted. (D) The typical absorption, excitation and emission of the obtained CDs. (E) The Fluorescent spectra of CDs scanned at different excitation wavelengths. (F) Normalized fluorescent spectra of CDs. (G) TGA spectrum of CDs and, (H) overlapping of emission of CDs and absorption spectra of AuNPs and AuNPs/PAMAM-Ab.

UV–vis absorption spectrum of AuNPs, AuNPs-Ab, AuNPs-Ab-PAMAM, ss DNA, CDs, ss DNA-CDs, and Ab. The inserted digital photo is related to the color changes in AuNPs, AuNPs PAMAM Ab, CDs-Apta, the CDs-Apta-AuNPs-PAMAM-Ab and AuNPs-PAMAM-Ab- CA125- CDs-Apta sandwich immunocomplex, the concentration of CA125 was 1ng/mL.

(A) The fluorescence recovery of CDs-Apta/AuNPs-PAMAM-Ab system after incubation with 5.0fg/ml, 9.0fg/mL, 20fg/mL, 60fg/mL, 90fg/mL, 9pg/mL, 90pg/mL, 0.05ng/mL, 0.4ng/mL, 0.8ng/mL, and 1.0ng/mL of CA125 Ag. (B) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and concentration of CA125 Ag. (C) Histogram related to the analytical signal of ssDNA-CDs/AuNPs-PAMAM-Ab conjugates after incubation with 0.01ng/mL of (a) CA125 (b) 0.01ng/mL CA125+5.0ng/mL AFP(c) 0.01ng/mL CA125 +2.5ng/mL CA153, (d) 0.01ng/mL CA125 +0.75ng/mL CEA (e) the positive serum. (D, E) The FRET response and calibration plot for CA125 spiked human sera.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of CDs/Ab in the presence of OVCAR-3 cells when incubating with Apta/PAMAM/AuNPs. (B) Calibration curves for fluorescence detection of OVCAR-3- cells when incubating with CDs/Ab for 30min followed by incubation with Apta/PAMAM/AuNPs (Concentrations of the cells is noted in related graph). (C) Cellular cytotoxicity assessment of the CDs using the MTT assay toward OVCAR-3 cells (All values are the average of triplicate measurements) (D) Laser scanning microscopy images of OVCAR-3 cells incubated with 0.2mg/mL CDs at 37°C, scale bar =20µm, typically incubated with CDs-Ab for 4h and washed cells are imaged under yellow (b)/ blue (c) and green (d) channels, part (a) shows the OVCAR-3 cells without CD-Ab and part (e) shows the MCF-7 control cells with CD-Ab under bright field.

The mechanism for the FRET-based detection system: in the first part the fluorescent CDs were conjugated to DNA; and then the CDs-Apta fluorescence response quenched by AuNPs-PAMAM-Ab through FRET in the presence of CA125 analyte.
Comparison of analytical properties of the presented immunosensor with the various CA125 Ag immunosensors.

head.ce:title: An amplified comparative fluorescence resonance energy transfer immunosensing of CA125 tumor marker and ovarian cancer cells using green and economic carbon dots for bio-applications in labeling, imaging and sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cellular and Molecular Reserch Center , Kurdistan University of Medical Sciences, 66177-13446 Sananandaj, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cellular and Molecular Reserch Center , Kurdistan University of Medical Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CA125, is a marker in the clinical diagnosis of several cancers and currently is the best serum-based tumor marker for ovarian cancer. Here, we developed an ultrasensitive antibody-ssDNA aptamer sandwich-type fluorescence immunosensor for CA125 detection. Based on a novel signal amplification strategy the carbon dots (CDs) functionalized with aptamer (CD-aptamer) used as detection probe and PAMAM-Dendrimers/AuNPs was used for covalent attachment of CA125-antibody and completing the sandwich assay method. By measuring of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) signals between CDs and AuNPs as nanoquenchers, the fluorescence signal quenched during sandwich complex formed between anti-CA125, CA125 and CDs-Aptamer and decreasing of fluorescence response signal is related to CA125 concentrations. Under optimal conditions, the immunosensor exhibited an extremely low calculated detection limit of 0.5fg/mL with wide linear range 1.0fg/mL to 1.0ng/mL of CA 125. The application of the immunosensor for CA125 detection in serum samples and measuring of ovarian-cancer cells was also investigated. The immunosensor revealed good sensitivity and specificity with ovarian cell concentrations from 2.5×103 to 2×104 cells/mL with correlation coefficient of 0.9937 and detection limit of 400cells/mL (4 cell in 10μL), indicating potential application of immunosensor in clinical monitoring of tumor biomarkers. Furthermore, the cell viability was not changed upon treatment with CDs probe during 24h, showing the low cytotoxicity of the probe. More importantly, CDs-antibody hybrid was achieved in selective imaging of the cancer cells over the OVCAR-3 line cells, implying its potential applications in biosensing, as well as in cancer diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer, the second leading cause of death, is a group of diseases involving abnormal cell growth with the potential to invade other parts of the body. The survival of cancer patients is strongly associated with the stage of the tumor at the time of diagnosis. Therefore, the implementation of highly sensitive and specific progression monitoring for timely cancer diagnosis is of great clinical connotation (So­gaard et al., 2013). To this end, molecular recognition probes such as monoclonal antibodies have significantly improved the performance of routine cancer diagnostics. The life-time risk of having ovarian cancer is 1 in 70 women's. CA125 that counting as the seventh cause of cancer-related death among women is a repeating peptide epitope of the mucin MUC16, (Florea et al., 2014; Gupta and Lis, 2009) which promotes cancer cell proliferation, and currently is the best serum-based tumor marker for ovarian cancer. Furthermore, the quantitative detection of CA125 in serum will be valuable for early diagnosis because ovarian cancer is usually asymptomatic until advanced stages. In recent years, a variety of immunological and biochemical methods, such as radioimmunoassay, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), (Scholler et al., 2006), electrochemiluminescent immunoassay (Xu et al., 2012) chemiluminescence (Chen et al., 2015), electrochemical (Raghav and Srivastava, 2015), piezoelectric immunosensors (Ding et al., 2008), and field effect transistors (Wang and Yau, 2011a, 2011b) have been developed for CA125 detection. Even though they have the advantages of being selective and sensitive, these techniques have the disadvantages of a high cost, long analysis time, the need for qualified personnel and sophisticated instrumentation; also their sensitivity is not high enough to detect tumors at an early stage. Apart from the inherent advantages of biosensors, aptamer-based biosensors as biorecognition elements in optical bioassays have provided novel cancer diagnostic strategies with superior performance for early, sensitive, and specific detection of cancer biomarkers and offer the advantage of reusability over antibodies. Aptamers, an excellent class of functional biomolecules have demonstrated significant potential as versatile biorecognition probes in early cancer diagnosis and considered as a great alternative to supplement antibody-based methodologies, with unique recognition and binding ability to their targets (Bohunicky and Mousa, 2011). This target-specific binding takes place through a structural recognition process which is derived from their capability of folding upon binding with their target molecule similar to the one mediating antibody-antigen reactions, and thus is often referred to as “chemical antibodies”.

Fluorescence detection because of its inherent capability for real-time detection and ease of labeling aptamers with fluorescent labels, the availability of many different fluorophores and quenchers is widely employed (Witt et al., 2015). Since aptamers have been selected to bind very different targets, of note, the dual-site designed binding assay, also known as the ‘‘sandwich’’ assay, is one of the most used reported assay formats. A sandwich immunosensing is a customized biosensing format in which the analyte is not to be labeled by it, but recognized by another aptamer or antibody loading the label for the detection. An alternative format is the use of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), which relies on the energy transfer between two fluorescent molecules–donor and acceptor (Bhatnagar et al., 2016; Wei et al., 2012). FRET noteworthy due to its high sensitivity, low background signal and fast sample analysis is one of the most important tools for biological studies specially to monitor protein interaction. Using of QDs due to their potential toxicity and high background signal for detection of biomolecules has certain limitations. Also about many other luminescent labels, such as organic dyes this problem is common. The solution to minimize the background signal is to use the excitation wavelength dependent CDs with additional advantages of sharp emission peaks and long photoluminescence (PL) lifetimes for biolabeling and bioassays (Zheng et al., 2015; Reckmeier et al., 2016). CDs, as a novel member of carbon family, compared with QDs, are gradually possess outstanding characteristics for biological applications because of their facile surface modification and preparation, biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, good stability against photobleaching and their high water solubility that allowing homogeneous sensing assays (Lim et al., 2015; Shen and Xia, 2014; Tan et al., 2015; Bhunia et al., 2013; Cai et al., 2015; Gupta et al., 2016; Barati et al., 2015; Hamd-Ghadareh and Salimi, 2017). Across the FRET detection process, Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) due to its unique catalytic and electronic properties and high FRET quenching efficiency is often adopted as an FRET reagent, which is beneficial for the increment of accuracy and sensitivity. Furthermore, for increasing the sensitivity of immunosensors, PAMAM dendrimers as monodisperse and highly branched macromolecules that contain active functional groups can be used to immobilize antibodies or aptamers ( Kavosi et al., 2014a; 2014b; 2015).

Here, in continues of previous report for application of FRET in biosensing (Hamd-Ghadareh and Salimi, 2017) a sensitive FRET-immunosensor based on highly biospecific recognition interactions between antigens and the corresponding antibody and aptamer has been developed for detection of CA125 tumor maker. In this sandwich-type optical immunosensor, CA125 antibody was immobilized on AuNPs. Meanwhile, to further enhance the fluorescence quenching ability of AuNPs, they were applied in combination to PAMAM-Dendrimer which led to the attachment of more antibody molecules and therefore obtaining a high sensitivity and a low detection limit for quantitative monitoring of biomarker. The fluorescence of CDs can be quenched by introducing CA125 in the presence of thiol terminated PAMAM-AuNPs. The experimental results showed that the fabricated optical biosensor has high sensitivity, low cost, good renewability, and easy to use and it has a great promise as a practical platform for CA125 detection in serum samples, ovarian- cancer cells detection and also cancer cell imaging.
The applied materials, reagents and instruments for characterization and fluorescence measuring reported in Supporting Information (SI) file.

CDs were synthesized using a modified version of a method reported by (Zhai et al., 2013) by using one-step hydrothermal neutralization as synthesizing method instead of microwave method. The related details reported in SI.

To produce the covalently bonded aptamer-CDs conjugates, the carboxyl group on the surface of the CDs was reacted with the aptamer in the presence of EDC and NHS (Witt et al., 2015). Generally, 1-ethyl-3-(3- dimethylamino propyl)-carbodiimide (EDC) (200μL, 0.01M) and N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt (NHS) (200μL, 0.01M) were added to the CDs solution to activate the surface carboxylic groups for 1h with continuous stirring. The resulting NHS cyclic ester is then reacting with an amine-modified aptamer to form a stable amide bond between the CDs surface and aptamer. This conjugation was confirmed by fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of AuNPs, and co-immobilization of PAMAM and anti-CA125 antibody onto AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Citrate-coated AuNPs were prepared by the citrate reduction of HAuCl4 based on an established procedure (Pan et al., 2005). Anti-CA125 could be labeled with AuNPs-PAMAM via reaction between NH2 group in PAMAM and NH2 groups in anti-CA125 using glutaraldehyde as linker. In order to immobilize more PAMAM-dendrimers on AuNPs, methyl mercaptoacetate is adopted as an environmental binder material due to its great reactivity in linkage between the AuNPs and the primary amine group of the PAMAM molecule. The thiol terminated PAMAM was prepared by reported procedure (Pan et al., 2005). The related detail reported in SI.

The human ovarian cancer cell line OVCAR-3 obtained from the national cell bank of the Iran (Pasteur institute of Iran, Tehran) was selected for the labeling by the Ab-CDs and were cultured by established protocols (the detail reported in SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and Binding studies of the engineered NPs
ce:section.ce:para: To characterize the size and morphology of the nanoparticles, HRTEM images of as-prepared CDs was employed. TEM analysis revealed that the synthesized CDs have a narrow size distribution of 2–5nm ( Fig. 1. A). Based on the particle size distribution histogram in Fig. 1A (inset), it can be seen that the as-synthesized CDs were uniform in size and possess a nearly spherical shape with a mean particle size of approximately 3.2nm. The inserted down side digital photo in Fig. 1A, related to an aqueous solution of CDs exhibits bright blue luminescence under UV lamp irradiation with a wavelength of 353nm (the right side). The FTIR spectroscopy was utilized to investigate the chemical structure of the synthesized CDs (Fig. 1B) and analysis of related wavenumbers discussed in SI. FTIR spectrum of PAMAM modified with thiol groups in Fig. S1 shows an absorption band at 2554cm−1, which indicate the attachment of thiol groups to PAMAM. The HRTEM images related to AuNPs-dendrimer and AuNPs are brought in Fig. 1C and its inset, respectively. As illustrated the synthesized AuNPs are uniform and after dendrimer immobilization their sizes are bigger. Fig. 1D illustrated the typical absorption, excitation and emission of the obtained CDs. Commonly, the optical properties of CDs are known to be governed by a combination of these factors: size, shape and surface state (Li et al., 2014). The UV–vis spectrum showed that the CDs exhibited a significant UV–vis absorption band centered at approximately 350nm (Wang and Hu 2014), corresponding to the optimum fluorescence excitation peak and should be attributed to the n-π* transition of the chemical groups on the surface of the CDs and a shoulder peak at 180nm (Fig. 1D) that is a typical characteristic peak of CDs arising from the π–π* transition of aromatic C=C bonds. The maximum excitation and emission wavelength of the CDs were 345 and 453nm, respectively. As detailed in Fig. 1E, in the fluorescence emission wavelengths of the CDs, for the excitation wavelength over 310–400nm the PL peak approximately stands consistent (at 453nm) which is in contrast to the most other luminescent CDs, exhibiting an excitation-dependent PL behavior only when the excitation wavelength is larger than 400nm. Respectively, by changing the excitation wavelength from 400 to 420nm, the PL peak shifted from 460 to 490nm. In contrast, as different energy levels associated with different ‘‘surface states’’ formed by different functional groups are responsible for the excitation-dependent-emission behavior, the recognized excitation-independent emission over 310–400nm indicates an approximately uniform and well-passivated CD surface. Fig. 1F shows the normalized PL emission spectra of the CDs with different excitation wavelengths from 240 to 420nm. It can be seen that the emission spectrum of the CDs changes from 430nm to 500nm. The TGA analysis result was shown in Fig. 1G and detailed of results for this analysis is reported in SI. As can be seen in Fig. 1H the UV–vis absorption of AuNPs (centered at 522nm) and AuNP/PAMAM-Ab (centered at 530nm) have good overlap with the fluorescence emission band of CDs (centered at 453nm), suggesting that an efficient energy transfer between them can take place. According to multipole scattering theory the size of nanoparticles was calculated and the concentration of the AuNPs was calculated as 1.38nM.

In Fig. 2 the UV–vis absorption of the prepared CDs, ssDNA, ssDNA-CDs, Ab, AuNP-Ab, and AuNPs-PAMAM-Ab in 0.01MpH 7.4 PBS are displayed. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs show a maximum adsorption peak at 341nm, which corresponds to the n– π* transition of C=O (surface state) bonds and an absorption shoulder located at 180nm, corresponding to π–π* transitions of C=C (core state) bonds (Yu et al., 2012). A characteristic absorption peak at 260nm is observed for the CA125 aptamer (Kuang et al., 2016). The aptamer-CDs maintained the characteristic absorption peaks of both the CA125 aptamer and the CDs, indicating the successful labeling of the aptamer with the CDs. The inset in Fig. 2 is a digital photo related of AuNPs, AuNPs-PAMAM-Ab, AuNPs-PAMAM-Ab- CDs-Apta, CDs-Apta and AuNPs-PAMAM-Ab- CA125- CDs-Apta sandwich immunocomplex, the concentration of CA25 was 1ng/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The FRET mechanism of CA 125 Ag detection
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly the protocol for CA125 detection shown in Scheme 1 using a sandwich assay with AuNPs-thiol terminated PAMAM enhancement comprises the following steps: (a) modification of AuNPs with PAMAM through covalent bonding using methyl mercaptoacetate as linking agent; (b) activation of NH2 groups using glutaraldehyde and (c) immobilization of Ab on the AuNPs-thiol terminated PAMAM; (d) affinity reaction with CA125 Ag and finally (e) addition of aptamer- functionalized CDs (CDs-Apta) for formation of sandwich immunocomplex. The CDs-Apta probe was mixed with AuNPs-Ab such that the CDs-Apta could be adsorbed on the surface of AuNPs. Fig. S2 disclosed the related response of different concentration of CDs, and 0.025μg/mL was the optimum value. The quenching effect of AuNPs on the fluorescence of the CDs-Apta is demonstrated in Fig. S3. As can be seen with increasing of AuNPs from 0.034 to 0.15nM the fluorescence intensity decreased, then the change of signal response was slower, 0.24nM was used as optimum value in sensing process. Although the fluorescence for CDs-Apta (Fig. S4.b) was more than that of the pure CDs (Fig. S4a), due to increased rigidity of the nanostructure by conjugation to DNA strands, then was heavily quenched by the addition of the specific target CA125 Ag (1ng/mL) in the presence of AuNPs-Ab (Fig. S4.d), and AuNPs-PAMAM-Ab (Fig. S4.e). Furthermore, the fluorescence was quenched by the addition of AuNPs without the anti-CA125 and CA125 Ag (Fig. S4c), indicating fluorescence energy transfer between the CDs-Apta and AuNPs in this FRET process.

The interaction of CDs-Apta with AuNPs-Ab and CDs-Apta with AuNPs-PAMAM-Ab results in nearly incomplete quenching of the fluorescence of the CDs-Apta probe through FRET. The quenched fluorescence could be efficiently increased by the addition of target CA125 Ag (1ng/mL) (Fig. S4 d, e), which is due to the affinity-based interaction between the CDs-Apta, AuNPs-PAMAM-Ab and CA125 Ag through specific reversible base pairing to increase the FRET between CDs and AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimized conditions for CA125 detection based on FRET assay
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental conditions for immunoreaction greatly affected the optical response for immunosensing assay. These conditions included the concentration of PAMAM-AuNPs labeled CA125 antibody in the incubation solution, incubation time and incubation temperature. As shown in Fig. S2 the 0.240nM of AuNPs, was chosen as the optimal concentration of AuNPs in proposed bioassay.

Then the immunosensor for CA125 was incubated in optimized 2–3 concentration ratio of CA125-Ab, AuNPs, and AuNPs-PAMAM incubation solution at room temperature for 24h and the optical measurement was recorded. Using the catalytic behavior of modified PAMAM-AuNPs, fluorescence intensity of the obtained CDs-Apta decreased gradually and then tended to a constant value with an increasing volume ratio of PAMAM-AuNPs labeled CA125 antibody solution. At a dilution of 1:100 of PAMAM-AuNPs the fluorescence reached a minimum value.

By applying the optimized volume ratios, then influence of the incubation temperature and time for the determination of CA 125 Ag by dendrimer enhancement procedure were investigated. The obtained results showed that this immunocomplex have the best response at the room temperature. The affinity reaction was investigated in different incubation times (5–40min with interval of 5min) between CDs-Apta- and AuNPs, CDs-Apta and AuNPs-PAMAM-Ab. For CDs-Apta and AuNPs-PAMAM-Ab without target the time period of 30min was determined as an optimum incubation time, while in the presence of the target CA125 Ag, the recorded feedbacks show a rapid decrease in the fluorescence intensity at the first 10min, however, the intensity tended to stabilize after 20min nearly longer incubation time did not improve the response. These time dependent experiments clearly confirmed the sandwich assay formation and indicated that the CA125 Ag had a stronger binding affinity to form the immunocomplex structure. Thus, 20min was chosen for both the optimal fluorescence quenching times in the subsequent determination of CA125 Ag.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance and specificity of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Affinity is the most important factor controlling the behavior of antibodies. The structural basis of affection progress suggests that a decrease in the conformational flexibility of the antigen binding site of the antibody occurred. Fluorescence response of the sensor in the presence of different concentration of CA125 Ag is shown in Fig. 3A. The plot of the fluorescence intensity as a function of the CA125 concentration is shown in Fig. 3B. As illustrated the decreasing of fluorescence response is proportional to concentration of CA125Ag in two concentration ranges. These results indicate the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-Apta decreased gradually by decreasing the distance between the donor and the receptor at concentration range 1.0–90 fg/mL with regression of R2=0.9851 and at concentration range 2pg/mL to 1.0ng/mL with R2=0.9925. The calculated detection limit of the proposed method was 0.5fg/mL (3S/N). The proposed nanoprobe has achieved one of the most sensitive sensors for CA125 Ag detection in comparison to other reported assays ( Table 1). In order to investigate the specificity of the prepared CA125 distinctive immunosensor, immunocomplexation reaction analysis was conducted to weight the feedback of the immunosensor towards its adaptive immune response with other cancer markers. As disclosed in Fig. 3C the response of the sensor toward 0.01ng/mL CA125 and samples containing 0.01ng/mL of CA125 and (CEA, 0.75ng/mL), (AFP, 5.0ng/mL), and (CA15-3, 2.5ng/mL) was tested under optimum conditions. It can be seen that the most serious interfering species at concentration ratios of even 50 folds or more did not influence CA125 Ag detection, which indicated that the immunosensor had satisfying selectivity for CA125 Ag.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CA125 Ag in patient's serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The conceivable application of the engineered FRET based immunocomplex was exploited to measure the diagnostic applicability and accuracy of the CA125 immunosensor towards detection of CA125 in the serum of ovarian cancer patients. The CA125 quantification results obtained by the immunosensor (with consideration of the dilution factors) were compared with that of the analogous samples measured by the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kit, as a conventional clinical method for the analysis of CA125. We found the concentration of CA125Ag in serum patient sample 430.0±5.0ngmL−1, which was in good agreement with reported value based on ELISA method in clinical laboratory (438ngmL−1). Furthermore, the application of the proposed assay for determination of CA125 in human serum sample when spiked with different concentration of CA125 was also performed (Fig. 3D). As can be seen similar to buffer solution ( Fig. 3 B) with increasing of CA125 in human serum the fluorescence intensity decreased. Furthermore, the relationship between fluorescence intensity and CA125 concentration in serum sample is linear with the sensitivity of 111.52a.u/ngmL−1 ( Fig. 3E) which is in good agreement with observed value for calibration in buffer solution 119.5a.u/ngmL−1 ( Fig. 3A). The results suggested that the presence of proteins other than CA125 in the serum showed no remarkable effect(s) on the sensitivity and specificity of the FRET-based biosensing of CA125.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OVCAR-3 cancer cells with FRET assay
ce:section.ce:para: The high fluorescence quantum yield, long-term conservation at homogeneous phase, high water solubility, and prominently the small size of about 3nm of the prepared CDs could be applied as a favorable characteristic of a reliable biomarker in cell labeling for making their diffusion mechanism quicker and easier. To make it more pressing we selected a kind of genetic human model, named, OVCAR-3 to demonstrate the successful labeling and imaging functionality of CDs for their potential applications in biomedical fields. Furthermore, it could be observed ( Fig. 4 A, B) that the signal decrements had a linear relation with the number of OVCAR-3 cell concentrations ranging from 2.5×103 to 2.0×104 cells/mL, with regression equation of F=−0.0219C+662.61 (R2=0.9937) and the calculated detection limit was estimated to be 400 cells/mL (4 cells in 10μL of injected sample) at a S/N of 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity evaluation of the CDs nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the actual usefulness of the as prepared CDs in cell imaging, the characteristic cell cytotoxicity of our synthesized CDs was treated by introducing CDs in OVCAR-3 cells as a test sample using the MTT assay [3-(4, 5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)−2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide]. To evaluate the cytotoxicity of CDs, the relative viabilities of OVCAR-3 cells exposed to CDs were measured by the MTT assay in 570/630 wavelength. As shown in Fig. 4C, CDs showed negligible cytotoxicity toward OVCAR-3 cells in all treated doses in 24h but higher doses (0.2mg/mL) showed cytotoxic effect in 48h after treatment but in 0.1mg/mL no significant changes have been seen ( means ±SD, n=3) P value <0.001 and 0.0001 respectively. These results clearly show that functionalized CDs can be used as nontoxic labels for cellular and in vivo imaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of CA125 Ag in OVCAR-3 cells
ce:section.ce:para: For optical imaging the OVCAR-3 cell images and fluorescence intensity were monitored (at reaction times 4h) by an Olympus FV1000 microscope. The OVCAR-3 cells treated with CDs showed no significant change in shape. A strong yellow, blue and green colored photoluminescence on the cell membrane and cytoplasmic area of OVCAR-3 cells indicated that the CDs entered into the cells and conserved their fluorescent characteristics in the cellular environment, while no photoluminescence is observed at nucleus. It is clear from Fig. 4D (Images b, c, d) that the rod-shaped OVCAR-3 cells can be homogeneously and successfully labeled by CDs. In Fig. 4D (e) the MCF-7 cell lines (Breast cancer) cultured and used as negative control (expressing a little amount of target) to verify the specificity of the bioassay more intuitively. As can be seen, the treated cells with CDs-Ab showed no significant fluorescence that certify the high selectivity and binding affinity of the targets toward the designed probe. The results seem to suggest that the cellular uptake of CDs is efficient, requiring only a short incubation time (3–4h). However, we can clearly see the fluorescence on the surface of OVCAR-3 cells for the specific binding of CDs-Ab, confirming the successful coating of the CDs-Ab to OVCAR-3 cells.

The development of a novel immunosensor for CA 125 cancer marker and OVCAR-3 cells based on FRET from aptamer-CDs to Ab-PAMAM- labeled AuNPs heterostructure which switched off by the specific interaction of CA125 Ag with aptamer and Ab was reported. This newly developed CDs-AuNPs-PAMAM-Ab nanohybrids were thoroughly characterized by different spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. The mechanism of CA125 sensing by the proposed nanoprobe is based on the sandwich formation of fluorescent indicator CDs and AuNPs labeled PAMAM-Ab quenchers. Upon capping AuNPs, intrinsic fluorescence of CDs gets quenched and addition of CA125 lead to closing of fluorescent indicator CDs to AuNPs surface and more decrements in the fluorescence signal of CDs. The proposed immunosensor exhibits very high selectivity and sensitivity toward CA125 Ag compared to other biosensors and the detection limit was 0.5 fg/mL at wide concentration range up to 6 orders of magnitude. Furthermore, the proposed assay can be used for detection of OVCAR-3 cancer cells at range 2500–20,000 cells with detection limit 4 cells/10μL. More importantly, selective imaging of the cancer cells was achieved by this CDs-Ab hybrid implying its potential applications in biosensing, as well as in cancer diagnosis. The low toxicity of the CDs guaranteed the activity of the cell and maintained the sensing performance and presence of CA125 Ag in OVCAR-3 cells is successfully imaged with a fluorescence microscope. Compared with previous studies, our results also presented a favorable examination of the OVCAR-3 cells with a large detection range and a comparable detection limit was achieved. So, the prepared FRET driven Aptamer- antibody based immunosensor would have the potential to be extended for the detection of other cancer cells with the wide linear range and low detection limit.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative and the Research Offices of the University of Kurdistan

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.05.003.
